pr.title: vi: Define CrowdConfig struct and rename Fighter_804D6500
pr.author: itsgrimetime

summary: Renamed the misleading global `Fighter_804D6500` to `gCrowdConfig`, moved its declaration out of the fighter header into `vi1202.h`, and introduced a typed `CrowdConfig` struct for crowd/announcer reaction configuration. The PR replaced many `M2C_FIELD(..., offset)` accesses in `src/melee/vi/vi1202.c` with named fields such as `kb_threshold_low`, `max_gasp_count`, `horiz_margin`, and `blastzone_y_offset`, making the crowd gasp/cheer logic substantially more readable. Review feedback pushed the author away from pointer math and toward creating a proper type, and the final change incorporated evidence from UnclePunch Training Mode map names, `lbAudio_PlayCrowdSFX`, debugger validation, and runtime field behavior.
what_changed: Updated `config/GALE01/symbols.txt` so `.sbss:0x804D6500` is named `gCrowdConfig` instead of `Fighter_804D6500`.
Changed the global definition in `src/melee/ft/fighter.c` from `UNK_T Fighter_804D6500 = NULL;` to `CrowdConfig* gCrowdConfig = NULL;`.
Kept the common-data load source the same, but assigned `pData[21]` into `gCrowdConfig` instead of `Fighter_804D6500`.
Removed the old `extern UNK_T Fighter_804D6500;` declaration from `src/melee/ft/fighter.h`.

Added `CrowdConfig` to `src/melee/vi/vi1202.h` with offset-annotated fields for knockback thresholds, angle modifier data, cheer/gasp limits, blast-zone margins, recovery-y thresholds, and proximity thresholds.
Added `extern CrowdConfig* gCrowdConfig;` to `vi1202.h`, reflecting that this data is used by the vi/crowd-reaction code rather than being fighter-owned data.

Replaced many raw `M2C_FIELD(Fighter_804D6500, ..., offset)` reads in `vi1202.c` with typed accesses such as `gCrowdConfig->cheer_limit`, `gCrowdConfig->max_gasp_count`, `gCrowdConfig->blastzone_y_offset`, `gCrowdConfig->fighters_near_blastzone`, `gCrowdConfig->horiz_margin`, `gCrowdConfig->kb_threshold_high`, `gCrowdConfig->angle_min`, and `gCrowdConfig->recovery_y_low`.
Left at least one field partially unresolved as `x24`, showing the struct was improved where evidence existed but not overnamed everywhere.
smart_moves: The rename corrected a misleading subsystem prefix: reviewers noted the variable was not fighter data, and the final name `gCrowdConfig` matches the vi/crowd SFX usage better than `Fighter_804D6500`.
The author used a typed struct to eliminate unreadable pointer math, directly addressing review feedback that creating a type is an essential part of decomp work.
The struct uses explicit offset comments and padding (`pad18[0x20 - 0x18]`) to preserve binary layout while allowing semantic field names.

The PR grounded the `Crowd` naming in multiple pieces of evidence: UnclePunch Training Mode map names for `CrowdSFXManager_*`, an audio function named `lbAudio_PlayCrowdSFX`, code behavior matching crowd gasps/cheers, and debugger/memory validation.
The implementation avoided overclaiming on uncertain data by retaining an unresolved field name `x24` rather than inventing a purpose.
Moving the extern declaration into `vi1202.h` made ownership and use-site locality clearer than keeping the global exposed as fighter data.
decomp_lessons: When a global loaded from fighter common data is only meaningfully used by another subsystem, do not keep the original address-based `Fighter_*` name if behavior shows a better owner; rename based on use and evidence.
Replacing `M2C_FIELD` offset reads with an offset-annotated struct is a major readability and decomp-quality improvement, even if some fields remain unknown.
Fixing argument/global types first and rerunning m2c can produce better code than manually massaging AI-generated or pointer-math-heavy output.
Runtime validation and external symbol maps can justify semantic names, but the final code should still preserve uncertainty for fields that are not fully understood.
Header placement matters: declare subsystem-specific globals/types near the subsystem that consumes them instead of in broad unrelated headers.
For config blobs, document field offsets and behavioral use cases together; this helps future decompers connect thresholds to gameplay/audio effects.
naming_conventions: Use `g` prefix for global data pointers with semantic subsystem names, as in `gCrowdConfig`.
Prefer subsystem/behavior names over address-derived names once evidence exists; `Fighter_804D6500` became `gCrowdConfig`.

Use lower_snake_case field names that describe gameplay/audio thresholds: `kb_threshold_low`, `kb_threshold_mid`, `kb_threshold_high`, `angle_min`, `angle_max`, `angle_mult`, `cheer_limit`, `max_gasp_count`, `horiz_margin`, `recovery_y_high`, `recovery_y_mid`, `recovery_y_low`, `fighters_near_blastzone`, `blastzone_y_offset`.
Keep unknown fields explicitly unknown, e.g. `x24`, instead of assigning unsupported semantics.
Use offset comments in structs, e.g. `/* 0x2C */ f32 horiz_margin;`, to make layout verifiable against data offsets.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Reviewer suggested fixing the argument/type information first and rerunning through m2c to get better output than manual or Claude-derived pointer-math code.
Typed `CrowdConfig*` locals such as `CrowdConfig* vdata = gCrowdConfig;` replaced `void* fighter = Fighter_804D6500;`, enabling field accesses while preserving likely matching structure.
Padding was inserted in the struct to align known fields at their observed offsets, specifically `pad18[0x20 - 0x18]`.
The diff also normalized some hex casing in decompiled code, e.g. `0xe` to `0xE` and `0x1c` to `0x1C`, though this was incidental compared with the struct typing.

Offset-to-field mapping was inferred from uses in existing code: knockback category thresholds at `0x00/0x04/0x08`, angle modifier at `0x0C/0x10/0x14`, cheer/gasp limits at `0x20/0x28`, horizontal blast-zone margin at `0x2C`, recovery Y thresholds at `0x30/0x34/0x38`, fighter proximity count at `0x3C`, and bottom blast-zone Y offset at `0x40`.
review_feedback: Ribbanya questioned stray files `.build_validated` and `orig/GALE01/sys/.gitkeep`; the author explained a precommit hook was not installed in that worktree and intended to prevent recurrence.
Ribbanya advised that fixing argument types and rerunning m2c should produce better results than Claude/manual output for this kind of code.
Ribbanya noted the variable was not fighter data but a new or existing type related to `vi`.
Ribbanya explicitly requested creating a type for `Fighter_804D6500`, saying otherwise the codebase would devolve into unreadable pointer math.
The author initially suspected blast-zone-related data despite the vi module location, then updated the interpretation toward crowd cheering after further investigation.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename `vi1202` functions around `0x80321900` to `CrowdSFXManager_*` or otherwise confirm the UnclePunch map names.
Investigate unresolved `CrowdConfig::x24` at offset `0x24` and its relationship to `max_gasp_count` and `data->x1C`.
Check whether other `Fighter_804D6504/6508/650C` globals are also misattributed common-data pointers that deserve typed names.
Search for remaining `M2C_FIELD(gCrowdConfig` or old `Fighter_804D6500` references after the merge to confirm the rename was complete.
Look for later cleanup of `vi1202_UnkStruct` and `un_80321900`/`un_80321A00`/`un_803224DC` names in the crowd SFX manager path.
